texts.orig: Graphene and graphene oxide materials have recently attracted tremendous attention for membrane separation applications, because of its two-dimensional structure and good mechanical stability. 1 -3 Single layer graphene is impermeable to gases, however, functionalized nanoporous graphene (NPG) with high surface porosity can selectively permeate gas molecules. 4 -6 Theoretical studies reveal that NPG membranes will have superior performance over traditional polymer membranes. 7 However, manufacturing sub-nanometer pores in the single layer graphene for commercial scale membrane separation will remain a challenge. Alternatively, functionalized graphene or graphene oxide (GO) membranes have been demonstrated as promising membrane materials for gas separation. Recently, a breakthrough discovery by Li et al. 8 reported size selective ultra-thin GO membranes with high hydrogen selectivity over CO2 and N2. Kim et al. 9,10 developed CO2 selective GO membranes. The hydrogen selective GO membranes were fabricated by vacuum  ltration over the porous support and a spin coating technique was used to fabricate the CO2 selective membranes. These results demonstrate that gas transport occurs through the interplanar spacing of the stacked GO sheets, which changes with di ff erent fabrication processes. texts.orig: Graphene oxide doped ionic liquid ultrathin composite membranes for efficient CO2 capture †
texts.text: Graphene oxide doped ionic liquid ultrathin composite membranes for efficient CO2 capture †

texts.label: text
texts.orig: M. Karunakaran,* L. F. Villalobos, M. Kumar, R. Shevate, F. H. Akhtar and K.-V. Peinemann
texts.text: M. Karunakaran,* L. F. Villalobos, M. Kumar, R. Shevate, F. H. Akhtar and K.-V. Peinemann

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Advanced membrane systems with high fl ux and su ffi cient selectivity are required for industrial gas separation processes. In order to achieve high fl ux and high selectivity, the membrane material should be as thin as possible and it should have selective sieving channels and long term stability. This could be achieved by designing a three component material consisting of a blend of an ionic liquid and graphene oxide covered by a highly permeable low selective polymeric coating. By using a simple dip coating technique, we prepared high fl ux and CO2 selective ultrathin graphene oxide (GO)/ionic liquid membranes on a porous ultra fi ltration support. The ultrathin composite membranes derived from GO/ionic liquid complex displays remarkable combinations of permeability (CO2 fl ux: 37 GPU) and selectivity (CO2/N2 selectivity: 130) that surpass the upper bound of ionic liquid membranes for CO2/N2 separation. Moreover, the membranes were stable when tested for 120 hours.

This could be achieved by designing a three component material consisting of a blend of an ionic liquid and graphene oxide covered by a highly permeable low selective polymeric coating. By using a simple dip coating technique, we prepared high fl ux and CO2 selective ultrathin graphene oxide (GO)/ionic liquid membranes on a porous ultra fi ltration support. The ultrathin composite membranes derived from GO/ionic liquid complex displays remarkable combinations of permeability (CO2 fl ux: 37 GPU) and selectivity (CO2/N2 selectivity: 130) that surpass the upper bound of ionic liquid membranes for CO2/N2 separation. Moreover, the assembly of GO sheets and the interplanar spacing is tailored by the amount of functional groups present in their basal plane. 11,12 These GO sheets are mainly comprised of oxygen-rich functional groups on the basal plane and carbonyl and carboxyl groups on edges of the graphene sheets. In addition, the highly polarized oxygen atoms in the carboxyl group act as a negative center which aids in stacking of GO sheets. Ultra-thin GO  lms were successfully fabricated by vacuum  ltration or spin coating of dilute solutions of GO onto porous support membranes. 8 -10,12 For large scale applications, the ultra-thin GO membranes prepared by the application of external forces generally produce irregular stacking of GO sheets which might generate nonselective defects. Alternatively, GOs are dispersed in polymer solutions to provide molecular sieving channels in the polymer a  er solvent removal. 13 -16 GOs are decorated by functional groups and can be well dispersed in aqueous media. Most of the GO -polymer mixed matrix membranes are prepared using aqueous GO dispersions. To fabricate GO -polymer mixed matrix membranes, the polymer needs to be soluble or miscible with aqueous GO solutions, but very few polymers are miscible with these solutions. Here we utilized ionic liquids (ILs) mixed with GO as a selective layer. Ionic liquids are tunable in nature, a variety of ionic liquids have been developed and utilized for CO2 capture. 17 -19 Many ionic liquids are miscible with aqueous solutions and high performance membranes are reported using ionic liquids. 19 The key concept of this methodology is the enhancement of the gas selectivity of the ionic liquid by adding GO nanosheets with molecular sieving channels. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Graphene oxide synthesis
texts.text: Graphene oxide synthesis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized according to a previously reported procedure. 14,20 250 ml of H2SO4 was added to 2 g of graphite  akes in a 500 ml three-neck round bottom  ask at 10  C and stirred for 90 min. About 10 g of KMnO4 was added to the mixture and stirred for 1 day at room temperature. Then 100 ml of H2O2 and 500 ml deionized (DI) water was slowly added into the above mixture on an ice cold water bath. The color of the mixture turned golden brown from green a  er addition of H2O2 and the DI water mixture. The resulting mixture was stirred for another 1 h and then  ltered. The  ltrate was washed and centrifuged three times with 10% HCl solution. Subsequently, HCl treated oxidized exfoliated graphite particles were washed and centrifuged with DI water. Centrifugation and washing steps with DI water were carried out until the solution achieved a neutral pH.

About 10 g of KMnO4 was added to the mixture and stirred for 1 day at room temperature. Then 100 ml of H2O2 and 500 ml deionized (DI) water was slowly added into the above mixture on an ice cold water bath. The color of the mixture turned golden brown from green a  er addition of H2O2 and the DI water mixture. The resulting mixture was stirred for another 1 h and then  ltered. The  ltrate was washed and centrifuged three times with 10% HCl solution. Subsequently, HCl treated oxidized exfoliated graphite particles were washed and centrifuged with DI water. Centrifugation and washing steps with DI water were carried out until the solution achieved a neutral pH.

texts.orig: permeation of CO2. The complexation of ILs with GO was analysed using FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of GO and GO/ IL are shown in Fig. S3 (ESI † ). The pristine GO shows the skeletal vibration of non-oxidized sp 2 carbon domains at 1623 cm � 1 and for the oxidized graphite domains bands show at 3300 -3200, 1717, 1218, and 1042 cm � 1 corresponding to COO -H/O -H, C ] O, C -OH, and epoxy C -Ogroups, respectively. The IL/GO complex shows additional new characteristic bands observed at 1555, 1393, 1163 and 1022 cm � 1 associated with the ring stretching of the imidazole moiety, which is indicating the possible intercalation of the IL in the GO sheets through p -p stacking interactions between GO and the imidazolium group. Moreover, the carbonyl C ] O band for the IL/GO shows a slight shi  from 1717 to 1705 cm � 1 revealing the possible exchange of cations of ILs to the edges of the GO sheets. Furthermore, the synthesized GO and GO/IL complexes were characterized using Raman spectra. Fig. S4 (ESI † ) shows the Raman spectra for GO, [EMIM][Ac]/GO and [EMIM][BF4]/GO complexes. The pristine GO shows the  rst-order G and D peaks at around 1595 cm � 1 and 1331 cm � 1 , which are characteristics of carbon materials. The Raman spectra obtained for [EMIM][Ac]/GO and [EMIM] [BF4]/GO show similar D and G bands, and these values are similar to that of pristine GO bands. However, the D and G bands of [EMIM][Ac]/GO and [EMIM][BF4]/GO are slightly broader than D and G bands of pure GO, further con  rming complexation of ILs with GO sheets.

texts.orig: The XRD patterns of GO and the GO/ionic liquid complex are shown in Fig. S5 (ESI † ). Pure GO exhibits a characteristic sharp (002) di ff raction peak at 2 q ¼ 9.3, with a corresponding d -spacing of 0.95 nm calculated using the Bragg's law and this distance is close to the height (1.0 nm) of a single layer GO sheet measured by AFM. For [EMIM][Ac] and [EMIM][BF4] complexed GO, the (002) di ff raction peak shi  ed downward to 2 q ¼ 7.9 and 7.7 with a d -spacing of 1.1 nm. This shi  in the di ff raction peak implies the complexation of the ILs with GO sheets. The GO and [EMIM][Ac]/GO displayed a surface area of 7.8 m 2 g � 1 and 6.7 m 2 g � 1 as obtained from the BET N2 gas adsorption method.

For [EMIM][Ac] and [EMIM][BF4] complexed GO, the (002) di ff raction peak shi  ed downward to 2 q ¼ 7.9 and 7.7 with a d -spacing of 1.1 nm. This shi  in the di ff raction peak implies the complexation of the ILs with GO sheets. The GO and [EMIM][Ac]/GO displayed a surface area of 7.8 m 2 g � 1 and 6.7 m 2 g � 1 as obtained from the BET N2 gas adsorption method.

texts.orig: performed. Table S1 † shows the single gas permeability of the PAN support and the PAN/[EMIM][Ac] and PAN/[EMIM][BF4] coated membranes. The pure PAN porous support membrane showed a CO2 permeance >25 000 GPU (1 GPU ¼ 10 � 6 cm 3 (STP) cm � 2 s � 1 cm Hg � 1 ) and the CO2/N2 selectivity is 0.8, indicating Knudsen selectivity. The ionic liquid coated membranes showed a CO2 gas  ux of around 150 GPU. The sharp drop in gas  ux from 25 000 to 150 GPU indicates that the support membrane pores were plugged by the ionic liquids. However, the CO2/N2 selectivities are quite low with about 20 and 22 for the [EMIM][Ac] and [EMIM][BF4]. These values match well with those in the literature reported for support ionic liquid membranes using [EMIM][Ac] and [EMIM][BF4] with di ff erent supports. 27 -30 However, when these membranes were coated with the PTMSP sealing layer, the CO2 gas  ux further reduced and we observed a dramatic increase in the CO2/N2 selectivity. The CO2/N2 selectivity of [EMIM][Ac] raised from 20 to 110 and for the [EMIM][BF4] membranes from 22 to 57. This clearly indicates that the ionic liquid unplugged pores were sealed by the PTMSP sealing layer stopping any viscous gas  ux. The sealing layer PTMSP is a well-known super glassy polymer with intrinsic microporosity. The CO2 permeability for PTMSP is about 18 000 barrer (1 barrer ¼ 1 -10 � 10 cm 3 (STP) cm cm � 2 s � 1 cm Hg � 1 ), however, the PTMSP has a low CO2/N2 (5.4) and CO2/H2 (1.5) selectivity. 31 It should be noted that the high CO2/N2 selectivity of PTMSP coated ionic liquid membranes is mainly due to the covering (sealing) of the ionic liquid un  lled pores. A  er PTMSP coating, the ionic liquid membranes showed gas  uxes in the order CO2 > H2 > CH4 > O2 > N2, which indicates that the membranes allowed the fastest transport for CO2 molecules and very low permeation for other gases as shown in Fig. S6 (ESI † ).

The above solution -di ff usion model can also be applied to the supported ionic liquid membranes. 27 Here, the addition of GO to the ionic liquids provides hindrance to the gas molecules. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusions
texts.text: Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, GO sheets were combined with high CO2 solubility ionic liquids 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate ([EMIM][Ac]) and 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetra  uoroborate ([EMIM] [BF4]) to fabricate GO/IL hybrid composite membranes for e ffi cient CO2/N2 separation. We found that during membrane formation the GO stacks on the top surface of the membranes and provides sieving nanochannels for gas molecules that leads to enhanced separation performance. The unselective but highly permeable sealing layer and the GO incorporation are the key factors for the improved CO2 selectivity. The fabricated membranes are stable for long-term separation application with extraordinary CO2/N2 separation performances surpassing the upper bound of ionic liquid membranes. In addition, the IL/GO membrane manufacturing process is very simple and easily scalable, which could o ff er great potential for use in CO2 capture processes.
texts.text: In summary, GO sheets were combined with high CO2 solubility ionic liquids 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate ([EMIM][Ac]) and 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetra  uoroborate ([EMIM] [BF4]) to fabricate GO/IL hybrid composite membranes for e ffi cient CO2/N2 separation. We found that during membrane formation the GO stacks on the top surface of the membranes and provides sieving nanochannels for gas molecules that leads to enhanced separation performance. The unselective but highly permeable sealing layer and the GO incorporation are the key factors for the improved CO2 selectivity. The fabricated membranes are stable for long-term separation application with extraordinary CO2/N2 separation performances surpassing the upper bound of ionic liquid membranes. In addition, the IL/GO membrane manufacturing process is very simple and easily scalable, which could o ff er great potential for use in CO2 capture processes.
